elements.sent-2411-002: Sheridan \,\cite{Sheridan-jmo92 we discuss the usefulness and validity of simple diffraction theories frequently used to determine and characterize optical holographic gratings.}
elements.sent-2411-003: Experimental investigations obtained in recent years highlight the correctness of his analysis which favours an alternative approach over the most widely used Kogelnik theory.
elements.sent-2411-005: Sheridan published a paper comparing three different analytic theories and a numerical approach of diffraction from one-dimensional volume holographic gratings for significantly off-Bragg re\-con\-struction \,\cite{Sheridan-jmo92 .}

elements.sent-2411-010: Kong \,\cite{Kong-josa77 and the rigorous coupled-wave theory as introduced by Moharam, Gaylord and coworkers \,\cite{Moharam-josa81,Gaylord-apb82,Moharam-josaa95 .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: The results are important when it comes to the direction of propagation of the diffracted wave and its phase \,\cite{Sheppard-ije76 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: While the outcome of his paper was disputed at the time of publication, recent detailed experimental investigations confirmed his conclusion and highlighted the correctness of his analysis \cite{Fally-apb12,Prijatelj-pra13 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: In this contribution we will discuss the aforementioned first-order theories, their differences and the applicability to experimental data.
elements.sent-2411-014: The aim is to provide a background as well as a concise hitchhiker's guide to data evaluation of one-dimensional holographic gratings that might be useful for non-specialists, e.g., engineers, chemists and those who make use of holographic gratings (astronomers, spectroscopists) in science and industry.
elements.sent-2411-015: We will thus elaborate on the way of characterization of holographic gratings by using diffraction experiments and the evaluation of relevant parameters from experimental data.
elements.sent-2411-016: We focus in particular on the angular dependence of the diffraction efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-017: A vast number of advanced books beyond the simple assumptions taken here are recommended to the interested reader \,\cite{Loewen-97,Popov-14,Bao-22 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: We consider the particular case of holographic gratings which are recorded by the interference of two plane waves with wavelength $\lambda$ (and same linear polarization state, say $s$-polarization) at a recording angle $2\Theta_B$.
elements.sent-2411-019: This results in a sinusoidal, one-dimensional light pattern with a period $\Lambda=\lambda/(2\sin\Theta_B)$ along the direction $x$ (without loss of generality) which is perpendicular to the bisectrix of the beams.
elements.sent-2411-020: When exposing a light sensitive material to this interference pattern, a periodic structure is generated therein.
elements.sent-2411-021: Generally, such one-dimensional periodic structures for light in a medium are described by either their permittivity or their refractive index which might be a complex quantity: \begin{equation}\label{eq:refinx} \tilde{n}(x)=\sum_{s=-\infty}^{\infty}\tilde{n}_s e^{\imath s G x}, \quad \tilde{n}_s\in\mathbb{C}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-022: Here, $G=2\pi/\Lambda$ is the spatial frequency and $|\tilde{n _s|=n_s\in\mathbb{R}$ the amplitude of the Fourier component for the $s$-th order.}
elements.sent-2411-023: We distinguish two different limiting cases: (1) pure phase gratings and (2) pure absorption gratings.
elements.sent-2411-024: In the first case we will end up with a real-valued refractive index \begin{equation}\label{eq:refin} n(x)=n_0+\sum_{s=1}^{+\infty}\underbrace{2|\tilde{n}_s|}_{\Delta n_s}\cos(s Gx+\varphi_s),\in\mathbb{R} \quad\text{for}\quad\tilde{n}_{-s}=\tilde{n}_s^*\in\mathbb{C}\end{equation} with $n_0$ the average refractive index of the medium and $\Delta n_s$ the refractive index modulation of order $s$.
elements.sent-2411-025: The second case results in an imaginary-valued refractive index which is related to the absorption constant $\Im{[\tilde{n (x)] =\alpha(x)\lambda/(4\pi)$ by: \begin{equation}\label{eq:alpha} \alpha(x)=\alpha_0+\sum_{s=1}^{+\infty}\underbrace{2|\tilde{n}_s|}_{\Delta \alpha_s}\cos(s Gx+\varphi_s), \in\mathbb{R}; \quad\text{for}\quad\tilde{n}_{-s}=-\tilde{n}_s^*\in\mathbb{C}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: For the general case of mixed gratings including a phase shift $\phi$ between them we refer to Refs.\,\cite{Guibelalde-oqe84,Sutter-josab90,Kahmann-josaa93,Carretero-ol01,Fally-oex08,Ellabban-oex06 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: These Fourier cosine-coefficients, the relative phases $\varphi_s$ and the grating period $\Lambda$ determine -- together with the thickness $d_0$ and the employed (reconstruction) wavelength $\lambda$ -- the grating's diffraction properties.
elements.sent-2411-028: Fig.\,\ref{fig:gratings shows a schematic of various holographic gratings illustrating important variants of the general profile Eq. (\ref{eq:refinx ).}}
elements.figure-2411-001:
\includegraphics[width=	extwidth]{Fig1.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:gratings} Examples for grating profiles. (left) Sinusoidal, pure absorption grating, Eq. (ef{eq:alpha}) with $\Delta\alpha_s\equiv 0$ for $s\geq 2$. (center) Binary, pure refractive-index grating, Eq. (ef{eq:refin}) with $\Delta n_s

elements.sent-2411-029: A typical problem is to find out the characteristics of a particular, given grating, which are:.
elements.sent-2411-030: To start with the investiagtion we introduce and consider an important quantity, which can be obtained experimentally as well a theoretically: the diffraction efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-031: From an experimental point of view it is given by the ratio of the diffracted light intensity into order $m$ and the incident light intensity \begin{equation}\label{eq:DEexp} \eta_{m,\text{exp}}=\frac{I_m}{I_\text{in}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-032: For pure lossless phase gratings Eq.\,(\ref{eq:DEexp ) is equivalent to the diffracted intensity into order $m$ over the sum of all diffracted orders, an experimentally quite useful definition: \begin{equation}\label{eq:DEexp_phase} \eta_{m,\text{exp}}=\frac{I_m}{\sum\limits_{m}^{}I_{m}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-033: For pure phase gratings with an average absorption Eq.\,(\ref{eq:DEexp ) can be written as (see Sec.\,\ref{sec:grating_type ): \begin{equation}\label{eq:DEexp_phase} \eta_{m,\text{exp}}=e^{-\alpha_0d}\frac{I_m}{\sum\limits_mI_{m}}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Here we specialize on planar unslanted transmission gratings which are among the most important geometries used.
elements.sent-2411-035: In Fig.\,\ref{fig:geometry the grating geometry as well as the diffraction geometry for the wavevectors is shown.}
elements.sent-2411-036: The term unslanted refers to the fact that grating vector and sample surface normal are mutually perpendicular, $\vec G\perp\hat N$.
elements.sent-2411-037: For further discussion we divide the space in three regions: the entrance region (R1), the grating region (R2), and the exit region (R3).
elements.sent-2411-038: Such unslanted transmission gratings deliver the major amount of intensities in the forward direction into region R3.
elements.sent-2411-039: In what follows we assume that the refractive index in R1 and R3 is homogeneous and $n_0$, thus no reflection effects from boundaries need to be considered.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth-1cm]{Fig2} \caption{\label{fig:geometry} Diffraction geometry for transmission gratings, see Ref.\,\,\cite{Ellabban-Materials17}. (a) sketch for mixed gratings with phase shift $\phi$ between absorption grating and refractive-index grating. (b) grating with arbitrary profile (and a decay along the $z$-direction, neglected in this paper). (c) diffracted wavevectors $\vec k_m$ in reciprocal space. Further not yet discussed notation will be given in Sec.\,\ref{sec:model}.}
elements.sent-2411-040: The typical experimental data are obtained by measuring Bragg-detuning curves of the diffraction efficiency $\eta_{m,\text{exp }$ either by rotating the grating by an angle $\theta$ around an axis $y$ which is perpendicular to $\vec G\times\hat N$ or by scanning the wavelength.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Both methods allow for determining the relevant quantities $\tilde{n _s,\varphi_s$ under investigation.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Standard diffraction experiments, however, only allow to extract information on the modulus $|\tilde{n _s|$, the phase-information is lost.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Advanced experimental techniques or evaluation schemes are required to retrieve also these values \cite{Fally-oex21 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Modelling the diffraction of quanta (x-rays, electrons) from periodic structures has been the subject of study for more than a century.
elements.sent-2411-045: First theories came up with the discovery of x-rays and their distinct diffraction features from perfectly periodic single crystals \,\cite{Darwin-PhilMag14a,Darwin-PhilMag14b,Ewald-adp16.1,Ewald-adp16.2,Ewald-adp17.1,Ewald-adp17.2,Bethe-adp28,Bloch-zfp28 .}
elements.sent-2411-046: The solutions to the wave equation - arising from either Maxwell's equations or the Schrödinger equation - for a periodic potential (a complex dielectric permittivity) were found.
elements.sent-2411-047: Those Bloch-waves which are consistent with energy and momentum conservation, i.e., obeying Bragg's law, impose certain conditions on the diffracted wavevectors.
elements.sent-2411-048: Typically only two modes are considered: the forward diffracted wave (zero order) and another one (plus or minus first order, depending on the combination of wavelength and angle of incidence).
elements.sent-2411-049: We call this the modal approach for two waves according to Ref.\,\,\cite{Gaylord-apb82 .}
elements.sent-2411-050: It is characteristic that each of the Bloch-waves is an eigensolution to the wave equation and propagates through the grating without change.
elements.sent-2411-051: However, there is an alternative equivalent, in some sense more practical, description for the problem of diffraction from a grating: the coupled wave theory.. Here, J.T.

elements.sent-2411-054: In what follows we will discuss several important approaches to the diffraction problem and point out J.T.. Sheridan's insight.
elements.sent-2411-055: In the 1930-ies diffraction of light from periodic standing acoustic waves was examined experimentally by Debye and Sears and explained in detail in a series of papers by Raman and Nath.
elements.sent-2411-056: They employed an approach based on the phase-changes of light occuring when propagating through a periodic (sinusoidal) medium.
elements.sent-2411-057: They first assume normal incidence of light, neglecting any dephasing due to oblique incidence \,\cite{Raman-piasa36 .}
elements.sent-2411-058: In the subsequent paper they include oblique incidence and arrive at an equation for maximum diffraction at certain angles $\theta_m$ which actually are defined by the grating equation \,\cite{Raman.2-piasa36 : \begin{equation}\label{eq:gratingequation} \sin(\theta_m)-\sin(\theta_0)=m\frac{\lambda}{\Lambda}, m\in\mathbb{Z}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Here, $\theta_0$ is the angle of the incident beam's wavevector $\vec k_{in}$ with sample surface normal $\hat N$ (as well as that of the zero order diffracted wavevector), and $\theta_m$ is the angle between $\hat N$ and the $m$-th order diffracted wavevector $\vec k_{m}$ (see Fig.\,\ref{fig:geometry ).}
elements.sent-2411-060: This is an application of the Floquet theorem Eq. (\ref{eq:Floquetcondition ) discussed later.}
elements.sent-2411-061: By considering path-length differences (phases) of light beams, Raman and Nath finally were able to deliver a simple and famous formula for the diffraction efficiency (relative intensities) of the $m$-th diffraction order $\eta_m$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:DE_RN} \eta_m(\theta)=J^2_m\left[\frac{2\nu}{\cos(\theta)}\text{sinc}\left(\frac{\pi d \tan(\theta)}{2\Lambda}\right)\right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-062: Here, $J_m$ is the Bessel function of first kind and $m$-th order, $\theta$ the angle of incidence and $\nu=n_1\pi d/\lambda$ the grating strength for a pure phase grating.
elements.sent-2411-063: Of course, there exists just a single Fourier component $n_1$ for a sinusoidal grating.
elements.sent-2411-064: For the original problem $\Lambda\gg\lambda$ and $d\approx\Lambda$ (``bending of light rays within the medium may be ignored provided the total depth of the cell is not excessive''\,\cite{Raman.2-piasa36 ) we approximate $\sin\theta\approx\tan\theta\approx\theta,\cos\theta\approx 1$ and the angular dependence of the diffraction efficiency (nearly) vanishes with a diffraction efficiency for the $m$-th order of \begin{equation}\eta_m\approx J_m^2(2\nu),\quad \Lambda\gg\lambda, d\approx\Lambda.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-065: This is a frequently used result when dephasing (angular dependence) can be neglected.
elements.sent-2411-066: It describes the diffracted power into various orders at the same time, i.e., diffraction occurs in a particular regime which then was coined Raman-Nath diffraction regime as will be discussed in Sec.\,\ref{sec:diffraction_regime .}
elements.sent-2411-067: A similar result can be obtained by employing the concept of amplitude transmittance functions in Fourier optics \,\cite{Goodman-05 .}
elements.subsection-2411-004: The coupled wave theory 1: Kogelnik's approach for two waves
elements.sent-2411-068: While this approach was applicable for quite a number of diverse diffraction problems from periodic media, it was not useful for example in x-ray diffraction from perfect single crystals.
elements.sent-2411-069: The general reason is that implicitely the first Born approximation (undepleted pump beam, no multiple scattering) is not valid for thick perfect crystals.
elements.sent-2411-070: The crystallographers code-word for diffraction in thick perfect crystals was said to be described by a ``dynamical theory'' rather than a ``kinematical theory''.
elements.sent-2411-071: As already stated above corresponding theories were developed in the early twentieth century, nicely summarized for x-rays in Ref.\,\cite{Zachariasen-45 , the classical book by Zachariasen, or in a more recent article \,\cite{Batterman-rmp64 .}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Interestingly enough, for light optics the relevance of this field became apparent only after the discovery of the laser and the increasing importance of holographic techniques.
elements.sent-2411-073: In the late sixties of the last century the off-axis holographic technique \,\cite{Leith-josa62 and its application in thick holographic recording media \,\cite{Chen-apl68 asked for a theory of diffraction from artificially created, one-dimensionally periodic, planar volume holograms (gratings).}}
elements.sent-2411-074: After a number of papers were highlighting various particular limited cases \,\cite{Klein-jasa65,Burckhardt-josa66,Burckhardt-josa67,Gabor-prsa68 , a major step forward was done by H.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Kogelnik in his celebrated and extensively cited article \,\cite{Kogelnik-Bell69 .}
elements.sent-2411-076: He gives a detailed derivation, based on a paper by Phariseau\,\,\cite{Phariseau-piasa56 , of the relevant equations for the diffraction power (diffraction efficiency) from volume holograms for both, transmission holograms and reflection holograms in the following important cases:.}
elements.sent-2411-077: The interesting part of his extensive theory is the alternative approach to the ``dynamical theory of diffraction'' which is called coupled wave theory.
elements.sent-2411-078: He limits his consideration to the case of only two waves propagating at the same time in the sinusoidal grating whenever the Bragg condition is (almost) fulfilled.
elements.sent-2411-079: These two waves are coupled via the periodic potential (grating) with a certain coupling constant $\kappa$.
elements.sent-2411-080: The latter gives rise to an exchange of energy between the amplitudes $S_0(z),S_1(z)$ (which he denotes by $R,S$ remembering the terms reference and signal beam from holography) of the waves during propagation.
elements.sent-2411-081: The theory boils down to solve a coupled system of linear differential equations for these amplitudes.
elements.sent-2411-082: For phase gratings the coupling constant is a real quantity, $\kappa=\Delta n_1\pi/\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$, and for absorption gratings an imaginary number, $\kappa=\imath\Delta\alpha_1\pi/\lambda\in\mathbb{C}\backslash \mathbb{R}$, mixed gratings finally have $\kappa\in\mathbb{C}$.
elements.sent-2411-083: In the frame of this approach the Helmholtz scalar-equation $[\vec\nabla^2+(2\pi n(x)/\lambda)^2]E(x,z)=0$ is solved employing the Bragg condition, Eq. (\ref{eq:Braggcondition ), and an Ansatz for $E(x,z)=S_0(z)\exp{(\imath \vec k_0\cdot\vec x) +S_1(z)\exp{(\imath \vec k_1\cdot\vec x)}$ in the grating region R2.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: This results in a system of two coupled, homogeneous, linear differential equations of second order in $S_0(z), S_1(z)$.
elements.sent-2411-085: To facilitate the solution of this task, Kogelnik disregards second order derivatives (slowly varying envelope approximation).
elements.sent-2411-086: This is justified whenever the coupling strength $\nu=\kappa d/\cos\theta$ is not ``too large'', which can be assumed valid in most of the relevant cases.
elements.sent-2411-087: However, this simplification also bears another, more important, consequence which is related to the wavevector $\vec k_1$, i.e. the direction of the diffracted beam.
elements.sent-2411-088: Actually, the simplfication results in a reduction of the number of boundary conditions from four to two.
elements.sent-2411-089: Thus the remaining boundary conditions lead to (physically) ambiguous results.
elements.sent-2411-090: Or in other words: a manifold of solutions exists which gives mathematically correct results, however, they might not be meaningful in physics.
elements.sent-2411-091: This problem of Kogelnik's solution was clearly stated already in the publications by Syms and Solymar\,\cite{Syms-ao83,Syms-oa85,Syms-90 .}
elements.sent-2411-092: Kogelnik's choice for the diffracted wavevector $\vec k_1$ is simply \begin{equation}\label{eq:KogWV} \vec k_1=\vec k_0+\vec G\end{equation} and is termed ``K-vector-closure-method'' (KVCM).
elements.sent-2411-093:
The resulting diffraction efficiency of the first order (for an unslanted, lossless transmission grating) then reads:  egin{eqnarray}\label{eq:DE_Kog} \eta_1(	heta) = 
u^2	ext{sinc}^2\left(\sqrt{
u^2+\xi^2}ight), 
u =  rac{n_1\pi d}{\lambda\cos(	heta)}, \xi =  rac{\pi d}{\Lambda\cos(	heta)}\left(\sin(	heta)- rac{\lambda}{2n_0\Lambda}ight)\approx rac{\pi d}{\Lambda}(	heta-	heta_1).\end{eqnarray}.
elements.sent-2411-094: Here, $\theta_1=\arcsin[\lambda/(2n_0\Lambda)]$ is the Bragg angle (in the medium) and in the last step we have linearized the Bragg-angle detuning.
elements.subsection-2411-005: The coupled wave theory 2: Uchida's approach for two waves
elements.sent-2411-095: An alternative choice for the diffracted wavevector was suggested by N.
elements.sent-2411-096: Uchida \,\cite{Uchida-josa73 , In his publication he focusses on providing a theory and resulting equations for the diffraction efficiency for an attenuated grating with an exponential decay of the modulation $\Delta n_1(z)=\Delta n_1(z=0)\exp(-z/L)$ along the sample depth.}
elements.sent-2411-097: This is definitely a very valuable contribution and the work is continuously cited for this reason.
elements.sent-2411-098: However, unfortunately his physically more favourable choice of the wavevector is hidden behind the former, in particular as the author himself remarks that {\em ``the present treatment for the wave-vector mismatch is somewhat different from that by Kogelnik [\ldots] However, no essential difference arises in the result of calculation.''}\,\cite{Uchida-josa73 Definitely, this is bare understatement as far as (1) the direction of the diffracted beam and the (2) off-Bragg diffraction efficiency are concerned.}
elements.sent-2411-099: Uchida's choice for the diffracted wavevector accounts for phase matching at the exit boundary, energy- and momentum conservation and is sometimes called the ``Beta-value-method'' (BVM) in literature: \begin{equation}\label{eq:UchWV} \vec k_1=\vec k_0+\vec G+\Delta k_1\hat N,\end{equation} where $\Delta k_1$ is found by the condition $|\vec k_1|=|\vec k_0|=2\pi n_0/\lambda=\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-100: Taking into account the geometry shown in Fig.\,\ref{fig:geometry we find \begin{eqnarray}\nonumber \Delta k_1 = -k_{0,z}\pm\sqrt{k_{0,z}^2-G(2k_{0,x}+G)},\quad G=2\pi/\Lambda. = -\beta\left[\cos(\theta)-\sqrt{1-(\sin(\theta)-G/\beta)^2}\right],\quad\theta>0.\end{eqnarray}.}
elements.sent-2411-101: The geometry for Uchida's choice is shown in Fig.\,\ref{fig:geometry (and on-Bragg for the first diffraction order, i.e., $\Delta k_{+1 =0$).}}
elements.subsection-2411-006: The coupled wave theory 3: general solution by Gaylord and Moharam
elements.sent-2411-104: Sheridan decided to compare the two simple first-order theories \,\cite{Kogelnik-Bell69,Uchida-josa73 to the second-order theory \,\cite{Kong-josa77 and the full and rigorous solution (rigorous coupled wave analysis, RCWA) \,\cite{Moharam-josa81 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: Gaylord and Moharam treated the diffraction problem rigorously by solving the Helmholtz equation in each of the regions, matching the corresponding solutions at the entrance as well as at the exit boundaries for both, the electric and the magnetic field components.
elements.sent-2411-106: The total field in the grating region R2 is expanded in an infinite Fourier series of waves with amplitudes $S_m(z)$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:RCWA} E(x,z)=\sum_{m=-\infty}^{+\infty}S_m(z)e^{-\imath \vec k_m\cdot\vec x}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-107: Therefore, this method is also called the Fourier modal method \,\cite{Bao-22 .}
elements.sent-2411-108: Note, that in contrast to the modal theory a single wave is not a solution to the Helmholtz-equation.
elements.sent-2411-109: The resulting system of an infinite number of coupled linear differential equations of second order can be transformed into a state space description which allows to find the solutions by seeking for eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a characteristic matrix.
elements.sent-2411-110: Unknown coefficients are uniquely determined by the boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-111: Naturally, the diffraction efficiency cannot be written in simple analytical terms, though.
elements.sent-2411-112: We just refer to a number of excellent publications for a large diversity of cases \,\cite{Moharam-josa81,Gaylord-apb82,Moharam-josa82,Moharam-josa82a,Moharam-josaa86,Moharam-josaa95,Moharam-josaa95.02,Lalanne-josaa96,Bao-22 .}
elements.sent-2411-113: By utilizing this general solution, the parameters relevant for the different diffraction regimes could be quantified (for a sinusoidal grating)\,\cite{Gaylord-ao81,Moharam_b-oc80,Moharam_rn-oc80 , the validity of approximate theories, their hierarchy and relation to the modal approach\,\cite{Gaylord-apb82 could be explored.}}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Fig4.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:Comp_RCWA} Angular dependence of the diffraction efficiency $\eta_1(\theta)$ in the Bragg-regime (logarithmic plot). Comparison between the rigorous approach (3-waves-RCWA, red) and the approximate first-order theories. (top) Uchida's theory using BVM (blue) and (bottom) Kogelnik's theory using KVCM (mint). Parameters: $d=40~\mu$m, $\Lambda=0.8~\mu$m, $\lambda=0.488~\mu$m, $\Delta n_1=5\times 10^{-3}$. Dotted black lines are the $\eta_{-1}$ from RCWA. Further information see text.}
elements.sent-2411-114: As a consequence Sheridan thus compared, from a theoretical point of view, both of the first-order theories with the second-order theory and the rigorous approach.
elements.sent-2411-115: In a diligent analysis he came to the conclusion that Uchida's choice of wavevectors is superior to that of Kogelnik's \,\cite{Sheridan-jmo92,Sheridan-josaa94 .}
elements.sent-2411-116: In Fig.\,\ref{fig:Comp_RCWA the diffraction efficiency $\eta_1(\theta)$ for the RCWA and Uchida's first-order approach (top panel) and Kogelnik's first-order approach (bottom panel) are compared (for a sinusoidal grating), respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-117: It can be clearly seen that Uchida's result (blue) cannot be distinguished from the 3-wave-RCWA (red), i.e. $m=0,\pm 1$, even in the far off-Bragg region, whereas Kogelnik's prediction (mint) becomes increasingly dephased for off-Bragg angles.
elements.sent-2411-118: For convenience the minus first diffraction order in the 3-wave-RCWA is depicted as dotted black lines, too.
elements.sent-2411-119: Even when neglecting this order could play a role in the vicinity of normal incidence ($\theta=0$) this will not be the case for ($\theta\gg\theta_1$ for which the KVCM is considerably mismatched, the BVM however remains indistinguishable from the 3-wave-RCWA.

elements.section-2411-004: Experimental observations: diffraction regimes and types of gratings
elements.sent-2411-122: From a pragmatic point of view one typically is interested to extract the decisive parameters ($\Delta n_s,\Delta \alpha_s,$ $\varphi_s,\phi,d_0,\Lambda$) from experimental data by employing least-squares fitting routines for $\eta_{m,\text{exp (\theta)$ using the easiest one of the above diffraction theories that allows to model the data.}}
elements.sent-2411-123: The classification and characterization of the experimental data will be the topic of the following subsections.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Fig5.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:diffreg} Angular dependence of $\eta_m(\theta)$ using the (realistic) simulation parameters $\lambda=488\,$nm, $\Delta n_{1,2,3}=(5,2,1)\times 10^{-3},\varphi_{1,2,3}=(0,\pi,0)$. (top two panels) Raman-Nath diffraction regime ($\Lambda=6\,\mu$m, $d_0=6\,\mu$m); (central panel) intermediate diffraction regime ($\Lambda=6\,\mu$m, $d_0=40\,\mu$m); (bottom panel) Bragg diffraction regime ($\Lambda=0.8\,\mu$m, $d_0=40\,\mu$m).}
elements.sent-2411-124: Let us start our discussion with the type of diffraction regime as theoretically discussed in Sec.\,\ref{sec:RCWA .}
elements.sent-2411-125: We can identify three distinct regimes, at least for sinusoidal gratings, for which diffraction occurs \,\cite{Gaylord-ao81 :.}
elements.sent-2411-126: In this case many waves propagate in the grating at the same time along directions (orders) with a variety of wavevectors $k_m$ having low but considerable amplitudes, see Fig.\,\ref{fig:diffreg top panel.}
elements.sent-2411-127: These permitted wavevectors $k_m$ for the waves which travel along diffraction directions at angles $\theta_m$ are given by the Floquet-condition for periodic potentials: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Floquetcondition} \vec k_m=\vec k_0+m\vec G,\end{equation} where $\vec k_0$ is the wavevector of the incident (and forward diffracted) beam and $\vec k_m$ that of the $m$th-order diffracted beam; for a grating which is periodic only along the $x-$direction, relation (\ref{eq:Floquetcondition ) is required only for the $k_x-$components \,\cite{Gaylord-apb82 .}}
elements.sent-2411-128: This is also called the ``thin'' grating regime.
elements.sent-2411-129: Here, only two diffraction orders with reasonable amplitudes propagate at the same time, the $0$-th and another one ($m$-th order).
elements.sent-2411-130: This is sometimes also called the ``thick'' grating regime \,\cite{Gaylord-ao81,Moharam_b-oc80 for which the diffraction efficiency is noticeable only for the zeroth and the $m$-th order which obeys (at least approximately) the Bragg condition: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Braggcondition} 2\Lambda n_0\sin(\theta_m)=m\lambda.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-131: In other words: for a certain range of angles in the vicinity of the corresponding Bragg condition with $m=+1$ the $+1$-st order is strongly diffracted; in another range of angles say the $-3$-rd order might be diffracted without noticeable intensity to other directions (except for the zeroth), see Fig.\,\ref{fig:diffreg bottom panel.}
elements.sent-2411-132: Note, that a non-sinusoidal phase grating with three Cosine-Fourier-coefficients $\Delta n_{1,2,3}$ and relative phases $\varphi_{1,2,3 =(0,\pi,0)$ was assumed.}
elements.sent-2411-134: To discriminate between the different grating types for a sinusoidal grating from an experimental point of view, one takes a measured data set, $I_m(\theta)$, and will be able to distinguish the following cases:.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth-2mm]{Fig6.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:gtypes} Angular dependence of the diffraction efficiencies of $\eta_{0,\pm 1}$ for a sinusoidal grating in the vicinity of the corresponding Bragg angles $\theta_{-1}$ (left) and $\theta_{+1}$ (right). The top two panels (a),(b) show the behaviour for pure phase gratings, the two central panels (c),(d) the situation for pure absorption gratings, and the two lower panels (e),(f) for mixed gratings. The latter cases depend on the relative phase-shift $\phi$ between the phase grating and the absorption grating. Gray lines correspond to the sum of all diffraction orders, black lines to $\eta_0-\sum_{m\neq 0}\eta_m$. Reflections from surfaces are neglected by phase-matching R1-R2-R3, i.e., assuming the same $n_0$ for these regions.}

elements.sent-2411-137: However, for the sum of diffracted intensities $\sum_m I_m(\theta)= e^{-\alpha_0d_0/\cos(\theta)}$ will always hold in the latter case of pure phase gratings.
elements.sent-2411-138: Classification and evaluation of light optical diffraction data from a one dimensional grating requires a number of steps which are concisely presented below.
elements.sent-2411-139: The first step, of course, is to obtain the experimental data required: the angular (or sometimes wavelength) dependence of the intensities $I_m(\theta)$ diffracted from the grating under investigation.
elements.sent-2411-140: To discriminate between the Bragg- and the Raman-Nath diffraction regimes, respectively, is easily done by shining a laser beam at perpendicular incidence on the grating and placing a screen a few ten centimeters apart.
elements.sent-2411-141: If more than the transmitted (or better: the forward diffracted) beam can be seen, diffraction takes place in the Raman-Nath regime.
elements.sent-2411-142: Detectors can be placed at positions of high intensities, i.e. at angles given by the grating equation Eq. (\ref{eq:gratingequation ) for further, quantitative characterization.}
elements.sent-2411-143: On the contrary, if no more than the a single beam is observed on the screen, a rotation around an axis $y$ perpendicular to the grating vector and the sample surface normal by angles $\theta$ has to be performed.
elements.sent-2411-144: It is expected that at certain angles $\theta_m$, given by the Bragg condition Eq. (\ref{eq:Braggcondition ), bright spots appear on the screen and the forward diffracted, zero order beam is attenuated (or enhanced).}
elements.sent-2411-145: Thus a first measurement of the intensity of the latter will give direct evidence for the related Bragg-angles $\theta_m$.
elements.sent-2411-146: A final decision on the diffraction regimes, in particular the intermediate regime, asks for a full quantitative analysis: $\eta_m(\theta)$ must be measured.
elements.sent-2411-147: Typically photodiodes with detection areas of a few mm$^2$ in combination with amplifiers are used to ensure a wide dynamic range of several orders of magnitude in optical power.
elements.sent-2411-148: Therefore, the detectors have to be placed at angles $\theta_m$ at which diffraction occurs.
elements.sent-2411-149: While this sounds trivial one has to consider that diffraction angles $\theta$ do not only depend on the grating period $\Lambda$ and the wavelength $\lambda$ but also on its detuning from Eq. (\ref{eq:Braggcondition ).}
elements.sent-2411-150: Note that only if the Bragg condition is fulfilled the corresponding diffraction angle $\theta=\theta_m=\arcsin(m\lambda/(2n_0\Lambda))$, which forms also the minimum angle $2\theta_m$ between the zero order and the $m$-th order diffracted beam.
elements.sent-2411-151: For off-Bragg situations the enclosed angles become larger with an excess angle for both deviations $\theta<\theta_m$ as well as $\theta>\theta_m$ \cite{Fally-apb12 .}
elements.sent-2411-152: From a practical point of view this might lead to a beam which moves out of the detection area and repositioning must be considered.
elements.sent-2411-153: To start with finding out the grating type from experimental data, we define fingerprints for each of these cases is as follows:.

elements.sent-2411-160: For diffraction in the Bragg regime, the most frequently employed configuration, with moderate uniform coupling constants $\kappa$ and assuming sinusoidal gratings the first-order theories \cite{Kogelnik-Bell69,Uchida-josa73 are sufficient to evaluate the data using Eqs.\,(\ref{eq:DE_Uch ) or (\ref{eq:DE_Kog ) reprinted here.}}}
elements.sent-2411-161: This is true for pure phase gratings as well as pure absorption gratings.
elements.sent-2411-162: Whenever an (exponential) attenuation of the coupling constant with the grating depth comes into play Uchida's publication delivers analytic solutions \cite{Uchida-josa73 .}
elements.sent-2411-163: When it comes to mixed gratings including a phase shift between the phase and the absorption grating again analytic theories were published with slightly different focus, sometimes making use of interferometric (beam-coupling) techniques at Bragg incidence or by the angular dependence of $\eta_1(\theta)$ \cite{Guibelalde-oqe84,Sutter-josab90,Kahmann-josaa93,Carretero-ol01,Fally-oex08,Syms-ao83,Russell-ap80,Montemezzani-pre97,Neipp-jpd02,Neipp-oex02,Ellabban-spie07,Flauger-polymers19 .}
elements.sent-2411-164: For high coupling constants, also called overcoupled or overmodulated gratings, a careful analysis requires second-order or rigorous theories \cite{Neipp-jmo04 : in a series of experiments together with a comparison to theories, the limitations of the first-order approaches were investigated by the Alicante group.}
elements.sent-2411-165: In particular the characteristics of the angular dependence of the diffraction efficiency for strong coupling, i.e. overmodulated gratings, was in the focus \,\cite{Neipp-joa01,Neipp-ao01,Neipp-jpd02,Neipp-oex02,Gallego-oc03,Neipp-Optik03,Neipp-oex03,Ortuno-ao03,Neipp-jmo04,Neipp-oc04,Neipp-Optik04,Gallego-oex05,Hernandez-oc06,Gallego-Materials12 .}

elements.sent-2411-167: For more complicated, still ``thin'' gratings, rigorous theories are required.
elements.sent-2411-168: This is even more important for the intermediate regime and a big number of cases which were not addressed here, such as: nonsinusoidal gratings, binary and blazed gratings, diffraction considering the state of polarization (TE and TM modes), conical out-of-plane diffraction,\ldots.
elements.sent-2411-169: Still this can be mastered using analytic theories and taking into account a large number of Fourier-coefficients of Eq. (\ref{eq:refin ), also including the phase-shifts $\varphi_s$ (RCWA).}

elements.sent-2411-171: After all the journey through the holographic grating landscape let us finally come to the announced comparison between the two first-order theories.
elements.sent-2411-172: Based on all findings above our goal is to make a definite decision on whether one of them is superior to the other.
elements.subsection-2411-010: Comparison between Kogelnik's and Uchida's approach: theoretical and experimental results
elements.sent-2411-173: One rather obvious distinction between the two approaches which can be accessed experimentally is the direction of the diffracted beam.
elements.sent-2411-174: This was recognized early \cite{Sheppard-ije76 but never tested.}
elements.sent-2411-175: Experiments on a volume holographic nanoparticle-polymer composite grating under rotations (in-plane) and conical diffraction clearly demonstrated that the direction of the beam follows the predictions given by Uchida.
elements.sent-2411-176: In his approach the minimum angle between zero and first order diffracted beam is twice the Bragg-angle for the standard in-plane configuration.
elements.sent-2411-177: As explained above, any deviation will lead to an increase of this enclosed angle.
elements.sent-2411-178: In contrast, the KVCM choice would lead to an increase for angles $\theta>\theta_1$ and a decrease for $\theta<\theta_1$.
elements.sent-2411-179: The latter is not observed at all.
elements.sent-2411-180: This therefore is a very direct experimental consequence and proof of the correct choice of wavevectors according to the BVM.

Nowadays, excellent photopolymeric materials exist with saturated refractive-index modulations up to $\Delta n_1=0.04$ leading to coupling constants $\kappa=1/4/\mu\text{m}$, therefore, mechanical thicknesses of a few $10~\mu$m lead already to overcoupling with a $\eta(\theta_1)$ in the second quadrant.}

elements.sent-2411-190: The KVCM on the other hand largely fails to reproduce the position of the minima, thus leading to large residuals \cite{Prijatelj-pra13 .}
elements.sent-2411-191: This experimental result confirms the theoretical one and demonstrates that the phase of the diffracted beam is incorrect with the KVCM wavevector choice.
elements.sent-2411-192: While it is obvious that over the last 50+ years the optics and holographic communities have favoured Kogelnik's KVCM-approach to describe diffraction from volume holographic gratings, it is evident that the equally simple approach using the BVM delivers much better results.

elements.sent-2411-195: Sheridan let me know to have been enthusiastic about the work of Uchida: ``I have always been a strong admirer and promoter of Uchida’s work''.
elements.sent-2411-196: He emphasized the subtle, however important, differences between the theories but still was realistic enough to note that ``there are no Nobel prizes here and the community – as you note - to have opted for the KVCM'' (e-mail by JTS in 2012).
elements.sent-2411-197: We therefore can and should conclude that one of the first-order theories, within its known limits, is superior to another, very well received approach.
elements.sent-2411-198: Thus we strongly recommend to make use of Uchida's approach in future.
